title: 23 Tahsas — St. Solomon & The Assembly
narrative:
narrative": "narrative\": \"The twenty-third day of Tahsas honors King Solomon, the son of David and Bathsheba, whose name in the Hebrew Shlomo is connected with shalom — peace — and who is venerated in the Ethiopian tradition with a particular fervor unparalleled in any other Christian culture, for the Kebra Nagast declares him the father of Menelik the First, the founder of the Solomonic dynasty of Ethiopia, whose line in the tradition endured to the twentieth century. Solomon built the first Temple of Jerusalem with a magnificence designed to be worthy of the divine glory it was to house; his prayer of dedication of that Temple — 'Will God indeed dwell on earth? Behold, heaven and the heaven of heavens cannot contain thee, how much less this house that I have builded' — is read by the fathers as the prophetic question to which the Incarnation is the divine answer: God will dwell not merely in a house of cedar and gold but in the house of human flesh.

Saint David the King, Solomon's father, appears alongside him in this day's assembly, for the two Davidic kings are inseparable in the liturgical tradition that sees in their lineage the royal genealogy of the Messiah. David, whose name in the Hebrew means 'beloved,' was the shepherd-king anointed from among the least of his brothers by the prophet Samuel; he was poet, warrior, sinner, and penitent; he was the man after God's own heart who danced before the Ark of the Covenant with a freedom that scandalized his queen. In the Ethiopian tradition, the Psalms of David are the very breath of the Church's prayer, recited in their entirety in the liturgical cycle; David is never merely a historical figure but a living voice within the assembly of the faithful.

Saint Joseph the Betrothed, the foster-father of the Lord Jesus, completes this royal assembly with a quiet dignity that contrasts with the splendor of his royal ancestors. Joseph, whose name in the Hebrew means 'he will add' or 'God will increase,' was a just man — the term the Evangelist uses to describe him, a man of integrity and faithfulness — who received the most impossible announcement in human history with obedience rather than comprehension. His dreams, his silent guardianship of the Virgin, his provision of shelter and warmth in the cave of Bethlehem, his flight into Egypt: all these acts are performed without a single recorded word in the canonical Gospels, making him the supreme model of faithful action in the absence of understanding.

The theological seal of this twenty-third day of Tahsas is the mystery of royal lineage transformed into humble service. David and Solomon give the Messiah His royal credentials; Joseph gives Him His paternal protection and the cover of ordinary family life. The Incarnation requires both the grandeur of the royal line that makes the Child the rightful King of Israel and the hiddenness of the foster-father's workshop that makes the Child invisible to those who seek to destroy Him. The God who comes in Tahsas comes both as the Son of David and as the son of a carpenter — and both truths are equally necessary, equally holy, equally willed by the wisdom that orders all things for the salvation of the world.
